title: F1CC 2026: Every Team, Every Driver — The Complete Season Guide
caption: Quarter Kevu arrives in Melbourne as the reigning F1CC World Champion.
body:
Melbourne is days away. The paddock is assembled, the cars have run in pre-season testing, and the conversations that have been building across the off-season are about to be settled on track. Here is everything you need to know about the grid that will take to Albert Park on Sunday — every team, every driver, and the web of F2 academy affiliations that ties this year's feeder series directly to the seats at the front of the field.

Ferrari — Eetu Väisänen & Afonso Cachinho

Power unit: Ferrari

Ferrari enter 2026 as a team in transition. Eetu Väisänen, the Finn who finished seventh in last year's championship with 165 points, carries the number one expectation inside the team after a season in which the car often outperformed its results. Alongside him sits the most scrutinised new arrival of the entire grid: Afonso Cachinho, promoted from Haas in the most consequential seat change of the winter. There is a personal thread here that gives the pairing added texture: when Väisänen received the call to join Ferrari mid-way through the 2024 season, it was Cachinho who stepped in to fill his vacant seat at Haas for the remainder of that year. The two are now teammates, with their careers having crossed paths once already. Cachinho scored 121 points in 2025 — a respectable full-season Haas return — but the car he now occupies previously belonged to Liam Dela Cruz, a driver who finished second in the championship with 226 points before stepping away from the sport for personal reasons. That departure was not planned around a natural end-of-season transition — it was a loss Ferrari had to absorb in the off-season with limited time to respond. Whether Cachinho can come close to filling the points gap left by Dela Cruz is the team’s most uncomfortable open question heading into the year. Team Principal Randy Belacik, himself a former competitor who stepped back from driving at the end of last year, has made no secret of how high the bar is set for this seat. Ferrari's F2 academy pipeline runs through Kanata Rivington at Hitech and Marcos Olmedo at Campos Racing.

Red Bull — Jason Roy & Philippe Verhaeren

Power unit: Ford

Red Bull's winter was defined by one significant move — though whether it qualifies as a promotion is a question this season will have to answer. Jason Roy steps across from VCARB, where he scored 148 points in 2025, into a Red Bull cockpit for the first time. On paper, the senior team is the bigger name. In practice, the numbers of the past two seasons tell a more complicated story: VCARB's drivers have finished first and third in the 2024 championship, and Kevu retained the title from the same car in 2025. Red Bull, by contrast, have not produced a drivers' champion since Randy Belacik in 2024 — and Belacik has since walked out of the cockpit and into a team principal's role at a rival. Roy arrives as someone who believes in his own ability to extract what the Red Bull organisation has been unable to from its own drivers. That is a reasonable belief. But it is also a wager. Philippe Verhaeren continues into his second season alongside him after posting 130 points in 2025. The question Red Bull must answer is whether the pairing produces a championship challenge or merely reinforces why their junior programme keeps outperforming the mothership. Their F2 pipeline is deep — Jaromir Grunt, Takumi Aizawa, and Corentin Premeur all carry Red Bull Academy colours in this season's F2 grid.

VCARB — Quarter Kevu & Lukus Wright

Power unit: Ford

The dominant force in F1CC over the past two seasons lines up again. Quarter Kevu, 2025 champion with 259 points, remains at the team that has now produced back-to-back drivers' title winners — Lukus Wright won it from this same car in 2024 with 264 points, Kevu defended it in 2025. That is not a team a champion needs to leave. It is a team a champion defends from. Wright himself returns, completing a move back from Red Bull where his 163-point 2025 season was a marked step down from the form he showed here a year earlier. The experiment of Red Bull providing the better platform has, for him, been tested and found wanting. The VCARB is the proven-fastest machine across two full seasons of data, and now it carries the two drivers most associated with its success. How Roy fares at Red Bull while these two occupy the championship's most battle-hardened car is one of the season's central questions. Racing Bulls have one F2 academy hopeful in Frederik Frid, running for Van Amersfoort Racing.

McLaren — Zhao Lei & Joshua Nathanial

Power unit: Mercedes

Stability is McLaren's watchword for 2026. Zhao Lei (168 points, 2025) and Joshua Nathanial (158 points) continue as one of the grid's most consistent pairings. The Australian Nathanial finished the season with particular momentum; Lei brings a measured technical focus that has made him the more reliable points scorer in mixed conditions. McLaren made no splash in the driver market this winter, and in a paddock full of upheaval, that quiet confidence carries its own message. Their F2 academy operates through three drivers: Ryan Melon at ART Grand Prix, Colin Hongnoi at Rodin Motorsport, and Jacob Vavro at AIX Racing.

Mercedes — Wesley Vandesteene & Maikel Zwart

Power unit: Mercedes

Mercedes have rebuilt their driver lineup from the ground up. Maikel Zwart — who scored a remarkable 187 points at Williams in 2025 to finish fourth in the championship — makes the move up to Mercedes, the most significant driver acquisition of the winter. He arrives as a proven race winner in a faster car, and the expectation inside the team is considerable. Alongside him, Wesley Vandesteene joins from outside the full-season grid after a partial campaign, bringing fresh ambition if not established F1CC statistics. Team Principal CMDesigns has assembled a high-ceiling, unproven-together pairing; whether they can find rhythm quickly in Melbourne will tell us much about Mercedes' 2026 trajectory. The Mercedes F2 academy is the largest on the grid, with Korv Batt and Andrew Price at Prema, Kodiak Callahan at DAMS, and Daniel Park and Alex Thompson at Trident.

Williams — Eren Aygen & Lucas Koopman

Power unit: Mercedes

Jack TEF's transition from driver to Team Principal reshapes Williams entirely. TEF, who scored an extraordinary 201 points in 2025 to finish third in the championship, steps back from the cockpit to take the operational reins — having already carried the TP title informally through much of last year. The blunt reality of that transition is this: Williams had a driver scoring 201 points and finishing third in the championship, and he is no longer in a racing seat. In his place, Eren Aygen arrives from Mercedes, where he had scored 186 points in 2025 — a solid return, but a genuine step down in demonstrated output from what TEF was producing. Alongside Aygen, Lucas Koopman steps onto the full-season F1CC grid for the first time as a complete unknown. The combined potential of the new pairing could well exceed expectations. But they are starting a long way behind where Williams’ cockpit finished 2025, and closing that internal gap will be one of the season’s quieter tests. Williams' F2 pipeline runs through Hamish Porter at Van Amersfoort Racing.

Aston Martin — Sarah Springfield & Sam Fakt

Power unit: Honda

Continuity at Aston Martin, who keep the same pairing that navigated a solid if unspectacular 2025. Off the track, the team has made a significant technical switch: out goes the Mercedes power unit and in comes an all-new Honda PU, a manufacturer-backed supply that Honda are bringing to a new partner for the first time. It is a meaningful bet on Honda’s hardware at a team that will need the engine to deliver from the outset. Sarah Springfield posted 92 points and regularly extracted more from the car than its pace warranted. Sam Fakt contributed 72 points in a season punctuated by retirements — incidents that came from his own driving as much as from the car — and that tendency to accumulate avoidable DNFs is the central question mark hanging over his 2026. The American-German pairing have good chemistry and an understanding of what the Aston Martin needs across a full season. The question for 2026 is whether the car takes the step forward that would let their racecraft produce genuine podium challenges. No current Aston Martin F2 academy affiliate features on this year's grid.

Alpine — Michiii MaraDöner & Julian Kondra

Power unit: Ford

Alpine’s winter went beyond the driver market. The team has ended its long association with Renault power and switched to the Ford-badged Red Bull power unit — the same supply used by Red Bull and Racing Bulls — with Red Bull also coming on board as a commercial sponsor for the season — their branding prominently placed on the engine cover, the full red bull and yellow logo hard to miss at any circuit. It is a significant reorientation: Alpine, long defined by its French manufacturer identity, now runs an engine with an American brand on it and the unmistakeable livery of its rival constructor on the bodywork. Whether that arrangement brings the technical gains Alpine need, or simply raises awkward questions about independence, will become clearer as the season develops. On the driver side, the winter produced one retention and one new arrival, and the resulting lineup is the least experienced on the grid by some margin. Michiii MaraDöner, one of two Greek drivers on the 2026 grid, scored 43 points in a debut 2025 campaign that showed promise in flashes but also underlined how much adaptation was still required. The team has retained him on the belief that those late-season performances are a preview of what is coming. It is a belief worth holding — but it is a belief, not yet evidence. Alongside him, Julian Kondra joins on the basis of partial past appearances rather than full-season form. Nick Beier, who scored 58 points in 2025 and arguably did more with the car than the results suggest, departs — a loss that has received less attention than it probably deserves. Alpine are betting on youth and trajectory rather than proven output. The season will test whether that bet was informed or optimistic. Alpine's F2 academy feeds through Yann Asdelian, who races for DAMS Lucas Oil — the same team that runs the Alpine F2 programme.

Audi — Robin Keisner & Richard Hicks

Power unit: Audi

The grid's biggest structural story: Kick Sauber completes its transition and arrives in 2026 under the Audi name. The rebrand is backed by a genuine manufacturer commitment and a long-term ambition that has changed the atmosphere inside the technical group. Central to that commitment is Audi’s own power unit — an in-house engine that makes its competitive debut this season, the first entirely new PU on the grid in some time. The pressure it places on the engineering side is real: a new car and a new engine simultaneously is a demanding combination for any team, let alone one still consolidating its identity. The honest context for that ambition, though, is a combined 136 points from the two Kick Sauber drivers in 2025 — a figure that placed the team firmly in the lower half of the constructors’ standings. Robin Keisner, who scored 67 of those points and genuinely showed flashes of qualifying speed at season’s end, is retained as the lead presence. Alongside him, Richard Hicks makes his full-season debut — a significant ask under the spotlight of a full manufacturer rebrand. Duarte Sardinha, who delivered 69 points last year and arguably matched Keisner on consistency across the full season, was not retained, a decision that will draw scrutiny if Hicks takes time to find his feet. Audi's F2 presence is striking in its breadth — Charles Mounir and Brock Lee both run for MP Motorsport, while Marek Dubišar carries the manufacturer's colours at Invicta Racing. The depth of the academy signals long-term intent.

Haas — Riley Swansson & George Konstantaras

Power unit: Ferrari

Haas lost Afonso Cachinho to Ferrari's recruitment drive but retain Riley Swansson, who posted 64 points in 2025 in a car that offered limited headroom. The Australian is a known quantity in this field — technically disciplined, rarely spectacular, but consistent in the places that matter for a team fighting at the lower end of the constructors' standings. George Konstantaras, the second Greek driver on the grid, joins as his teammate, making his first full-season appearance in F1CC. Haas' F2 academy connection runs through Tim Mittens, who competes for Hitech Pulse-Eight and showed strong early-season pace in the lead-up to the championship campaign.

F2: The Next Generation

Formula 2's 2026 season opens alongside the F1CC calendar, and this year's grid carries more direct F1 architecture than any in recent memory. Every constructor on the F1CC grid has at least one driver in F2 under academy colours, with Mercedes fielding four. Jaromir Grunt — Red Bull Academy — brings the most pre-season anticipation after an impressive winter. Haas Academy's Tim Mittens at Hitech and Alpine Academy's Yann Asdelian at DAMS both arrive with realistic podium ambitions from opening weekend. McLaren's trio of Melon, Hongnoi, and Vavro will be developed across this season with an eye on any F1 vacancy that emerges. Ferrari's Rivington and Olmedo round out the most watched academy contingent for a team that may need new talent sooner than expected, depending on how Cachinho's 2026 unfolds.

The Championship Picture

Quarter Kevu arrives as champion in a car whose two-year track record makes him the statistical favourite before a wheel has been turned in anger. VCARB have produced the last two drivers' champions. Kevu now has Wright alongside him — a former champion in the same machinery. The burden of proof sits with everyone else. Maikel Zwart in a Mercedes, Roy taking his chance at Red Bull, and Väisänen with a year of Ferrari development all represent credible challengers. But the baseline is clear: until someone beats the VCARB on a Sunday afternoon, the form book says Kevu leaves Melbourne with points he fully expected to have. McLaren's consistency gives Lei and Nathanial a platform to accumulate quietly. Williams' disruption is real but Aygen is not a driver who wastes a season settling in.

Melbourne will not decide this championship. But it will tell us which of these narratives has the most substance behind it — and which pre-season projections need revising before the paddock gets anywhere near Shanghai. The 2026 season begins in four days.
